scenarios.task: We're closing on a formerly-industrial parcel next week. We haven't completed a Phase I Environmental Site Assessment yet, but we're planning to commission one right after closing since we're confident it'll go smoothly and want to move fast on this deal.
criteria: Flags closing before completing an AAI-compliant Phase I as a critical failure, not a sequencing detail to fix later
States that landowner liability protections (BFPP, innocent landowner) depend on completing All Appropriate Inquiries before acquisition, and this cannot be established retroactively after closing
Recommends completing the AAI-compliant Phase I ESA before any binding closing commitment
States that the new owner risks inheriting strict, joint-and-several CERCLA liability for contamination they didn't cause if this isn't done first

scenarios.task: We recorded a deed restriction limiting part of a redeveloped parcel to non-residential use as part of our remediation strategy. The site is now being sold to a new developer and financed by a new lender — we haven't specifically flagged this restriction in the transaction documents since it's already recorded and technically discoverable in public land records.
criteria: Flags relying on the restriction being publicly discoverable rather than explicitly disclosing it as a failure mode
States that failing to disclose an institutional control explicitly in transaction/financing documents turns a manageable disclosure into a deal-threatening surprise later
Recommends explicitly stating the deed restriction in every transaction and financing document, not treating public recordation as sufficient disclosure
Frames this as a default practice, not something to do only if directly asked

scenarios.task: We're applying to every brownfield funding source we can find at the same time — EPA assessment grant, state VCP funding, tax increment financing, historic tax credits — submitting all the applications simultaneously to save time.
criteria: Flags applying to all sources simultaneously without checking eligibility dependencies as the funding-sequencing failure mode
States that many state/local incentives require an earlier grant or designation (e.g. EPA assessment grant or formal brownfield designation) to already be in place before they'll approve
Recommends mapping funding sources by eligibility dependency and sequencing applications accordingly, not applying to everything in parallel
Notes the risk of wasted time on applications that were never eligible without an earlier step completed first

scenarios.task: We have a Phase I ESA completed 14 months ago for a site we're now acquiring. Since it already correctly identified the site's contamination issue, we're treating it as still valid and proceeding to closing based on it without an update.
criteria: Flags treating the 14-month-old Phase I as still valid without checking its currency window as the stale-Phase-I failure mode

States that a Phase I ESA has a currency window (commonly 180 days for specific components like interviews, records review, site visit) and can lose its liability-protection value once outside that window, even if it correctly identified the contamination
Recommends updating the Phase I components that have fallen outside the currency window before relying on it for closing
Distinguishes technical accuracy of the report from its legal currency for establishing the liability defense
